nodes.label: Agent Admin UI — live-editable agent config + instructions with a working chat preview (TKT-0039)
nodes.justify: grouping — the ticket root; subsystems are the layout shell, the config data model, the three panes, and the live-wiring slice that connects edits to the canvas

nodes.label: layout shell
nodes.justify: grouping — the ui-split composition assembling the panes

nodes.label: split-container wiring — ui-split hosting N resizable panes (pane count/grouping pending Fork 2)
config schema — what an 'agent' means here is Fork 1; candidate shape: name, model/provider, system prompt/instructions, generation params, tools
config store — session-held instance behind ADR-0120's store-adapter seam (persistence is Fork 3)
settings/options UI — ui-settings (M4, schema-driven) rendering the config schema as a form
instructions editor — structured long-form text editing surface for the system prompt/instructions field
canvas composition — ui-conversation + ui-surface-host (M2, ADR-0129) reflecting the current config
live-apply wiring
nodes.justify: grouping — the mechanism connecting edits to the canvas without a manual reload

nodes.label: propagation — an edited config/prompt reaches the canvas's next turn
turn-loop ownership — the app drives the turn loop itself; ui-conversation owns no transport (ADR-0129 clause held)
apply-time validation — a bad edit is rejected before it reaches a live turn, never silently applied

actions.label: lay out N resizable panes via ui-split
model an agent's configuration as a typed, schema-shaped data structure
hold that config in session state behind a store-adapter seam
render settings/options as a schema-driven form
edit an agent's instructions/system prompt as structured long-form text
render a live chat surface reflecting the current agent config
propagate an edited config/prompt into the next chat turn without a manual reload
drive the chat turn loop from the app itself, never handing transport ownership to the composed primitives
validate an edited config against its schema before it reaches a live turn

edges.why: a store needs a schema to hold instances of before it can exist meaningfully
the settings UI is schema-driven (ui-settings) — it renders FROM the schema, not the reverse
validation checks an edit against the schema; the schema must exist first
propagation reads the current edited value from the store
an edited prompt is one of the values that propagates
an edited setting is one of the values that propagates
an edit must pass validation before propagation applies it — fail-closed, not fail-open
the chat canvas receives the propagated, validated config on its next turn
the shell composes the actual pane elements; the settings pane must exist before the split-container can host it
same assembly dependency for the prompts pane
same assembly dependency for the chat canvas pane
